canvas.safeZoneLabel: Bottom-right PiP reserved for recording / camera

slides.title: Hello and Basic Courtesy
speakerNotes: Open with the first five seconds of meeting someone in Thailand and show how a tiny set of polite lines already changes the whole interaction.
Greet politely, thank and apologise, choose a safe polite ending, and handle simple yes/no responses in a friendly first encounter.

textBlocks.heading: Lesson focus
lines: You can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter.
Deliver a 2-line greeting exchange with the correct polite ending.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Introduce the lesson clearly and set the recording tone.
teachingVisuals: Hello and Basic Courtesy
First Contact and Courtesy
teacherCues: Use the deck title as the recording opener.
visualStrategy.rationale: The opener should establish identity and objective without visual clutter.

slides.title: What you will learn
speakerNotes: Learner can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter.
Learner can recycle recent vocabulary while using basic affirmation and negation in short controlled output.
Deliver a 2-line greeting exchange with the correct polite ending.
Catch greetings, thanks, apologies, and polite particles in slow speech.

textBlocks.heading: Objectives
lines: Learner can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter.
Learner can recycle recent vocabulary while using basic affirmation and negation in short controlled output.
Deliver a 2-line greeting exchange with the correct polite ending.
Catch greetings, thanks, apologies, and polite particles in slow speech.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Make the lesson goals visible before the teaching slides begin.
teachingVisuals: Learner can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter.
Learner can recycle recent vocabulary while using basic affirmation and negation in short controlled output.
Deliver a 2-line greeting exchange with the correct polite ending.
Catch greetings, thanks, apologies, and polite particles in slow speech.
teacherCues: Keep the slide readable from a distance.
visualStrategy.rationale: Objectives are clearer as cards than as decorative visuals.

slides.title: Say hello the Thai way
speakerNotes: Let's start with the first thing you will say in Thai: สวัสดี sà-wàt-dii. Listen first. สวัสดี.
Now say it with me: สวัสดี. Good. This is your safe, polite hello in any first meeting.
In real Thai, a greeting sounds more complete when you finish with a polite ending. If you are male, say สวัสดีครับ sà-wàt-dii khráp. If you are female, say สวัสดีค่ะ sà-wàt-dii khâ.
So learn สวัสดี as the body, and ครับ or ค่ะ as the ending that tells the listener who is speaking.

textBlocks.heading: Key language
lines: สวัสดี | sà-wàt-dii | hello
สวัสดีครับ | sà-wàt-dii khráp | hello (male speaker)
สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | hello (female speaker)

textBlocks.heading: Practice
lines: Listen and repeat three times: สวัสดี, สวัสดีครับ, สวัสดีค่ะ.
Your turn: I will point to a male or female role. Say the correct full greeting aloud before I show the answer.
Substitution drill: replace the ending — say สวัสดี with ครับ, now with ค่ะ.

thaiFocus.thai: สวัสดีครับ

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Show the greeting stack clearly with one base form and two polite full versions.
teachingVisuals: Large Thai script for สวัสดี with transliteration and English underneath.
Two smaller follow-up cards for สวัสดีครับ and สวัสดีค่ะ.
teacherCues: Pause after the base form before adding the polite ending versions.
Model the greeting naturally — you should hear it before reading it.
visualStrategy.rationale: The greeting line itself is the teaching target, so a photograph would add clutter without helping recall.

slides.title: Thank, apologise, and soften the moment
speakerNotes: These next courtesy phrases do different social jobs. Listen first to all three: ขอบคุณ khàawp-khun, ขอโทษ khǎaw-thôot, ไม่เป็นไร mâi bpen rai.
ขอบคุณ closes a small moment warmly — after help, service, or kindness. Someone holds the door? ขอบคุณครับ.
ขอโทษ opens a repair. It helps you interrupt gently or apologise without crashing into the conversation. Need to get past someone? ขอโทษครับ.
ไม่เป็นไร smooths the moment afterwards. Someone says ขอโทษ to you? You say ไม่เป็นไร — it is okay, no problem, never mind.
Notice that สวัสดี opens the meeting, ขอบคุณ closes a kind moment, and ขอโทษ repairs a bump. Three lines, three social jobs.

textBlocks.heading: Key language
lines: ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | thank you
ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | excuse me / sorry
ไม่เป็นไร | mâi bpen rai | it is okay / no problem

textBlocks.heading: Practice
lines: Discrimination drill: I describe a situation. Choose ขอบคุณ or ขอโทษ — which one fits? Say it aloud.
Response-building drill: I say ขอโทษครับ. Your turn — what do you say back? Pause and answer before I show it.
Substitution drill: say ขอบคุณ with ครับ, now with ค่ะ. Now say ขอโทษ with ครับ, now with ค่ะ.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Contrast thanking, apologising, and calming the moment without overloading the frame.
teachingVisuals: Two-column board: thank/close versus interrupt/repair.
Short arrow from ขอบคุณ to ไม่เป็นไร to show the natural reply pattern.
teacherCues: Explain function before translation so you hear the social job of each phrase.
Recycle สวัสดี in the example: 'After สวัสดี, things go well — ขอบคุณ. If something goes wrong — ขอโทษ.'
visualStrategy.rationale: This section teaches social function and response pattern, which is clearer as a contrast board than as a photo.

slides.title: Pick the right polite ending
speakerNotes: Polite endings are not decoration. Think of them as the final signal telling the listener how your line should land.
ครับ khráp is the common male polite ending. ค่ะ khâ is the common female ending for statements. คะ khá is the lighter female ending often heard with questions.
For this lesson, keep one safe beginner rule: learn ครับ and ค่ะ first for polite statements, and notice คะ as the question partner rather than forcing it into every line.
Now let's practise. Add ครับ or ค่ะ to every phrase you already know. สวัสดีครับ. ขอบคุณค่ะ. ขอโทษครับ. ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ.

textBlocks.heading: Key language
lines: ครับ | khráp | male polite ending
ค่ะ | khâ | female statement ending
คะ | khá | female question ending

textBlocks.heading: Practice
lines: Substitution drill: I say a bare phrase. You add the correct polite ending. สวัสดี — your turn. ขอบคุณ — your turn. ขอโทษ — your turn.
Discrimination drill: I say ค่ะ or คะ. Is the speaker making a statement or asking a question? Choose before I tell you.
Substitution drill round 2: now try it with ไม่เป็นไร and ใช่. Add ครับ or ค่ะ.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Show the three polite endings as a usable choice system with simple examples.
teachingVisuals: Three stacked chips for ครับ, ค่ะ, and คะ with one-line explanations.
A short substitution line showing the greeting changing with the polite ending.
teacherCues: Keep the explanation practical. One rule, then practice immediately.
Recycle ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ from section 2 in the substitution examples.
visualStrategy.rationale: You need a functional choice system here, so labelled chips and drill prompts teach better than imagery.

slides.title: Answer yes, no, and no problem naturally
speakerNotes: Now add two response tools: ใช่ châi and ไม่ใช่ mâi châi.
ใช่ and ไม่ใช่ answer the idea. They tell someone yes, that is right, or no, that is not right. You already know ไม่เป็นไร — that does a different job. It manages the feeling of the moment.
That difference matters. ใช่ answers the question. ไม่เป็นไร answers the emotion. Otherwise you may answer correctly but still sound strange.
Let's see all your tools together now. สวัสดี opens the meeting. ขอบคุณ closes a kind moment. ขอโทษ repairs a bump. ใช่ and ไม่ใช่ answer the idea. ไม่เป็นไร softens everything.

textBlocks.heading: Key language
lines: ใช่ | châi | yes / that is right
ไม่ใช่ | mâi châi | no / not right
ไม่เป็นไร | mâi bpen rai | it is okay / no problem

textBlocks.heading: Practice
lines: Pause-and-produce: I ask a yes/no question. Say ใช่ or ไม่ใช่ before I show the answer.
Response-building: I say ขอโทษครับ. What do you say back? Then I say ขอบคุณค่ะ. What do you say back?
Substitution drill: say ใช่ with ครับ, now with ค่ะ. Say ไม่ใช่ with ครับ, now with ค่ะ.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Show the difference between a direct answer and a social softener inside one compact response ladder.
teachingVisuals: A three-step response ladder: ใช่ / ไม่ใช่ / ไม่เป็นไร.
One mini-scenario showing apology followed by ไม่เป็นไร, and question followed by ใช่.
teacherCues: Explain that ไม่เป็นไร is not a yes/no answer — it is an emotional softener.
Recycle all previous vocab: สวัสดี, ขอบคุณ, ขอโทษ, ครับ/ค่ะ in examples.
visualStrategy.rationale: This is a response-meaning distinction, so a compact dialogue ladder is clearer than any supporting image.

slides.title: Pronunciation: hearing Thai tones for the first time
speakerNotes: Before we roleplay, let's train your ear. Thai is a tone language — the pitch of your voice changes the meaning of a word.
You have already heard two different tones in this lesson. Listen: khráp — that is a high tone, it rises up sharply. Now listen: khâ — that is a falling tone, it starts high and drops down.
Try it. Say khráp with your voice going up. Now say khâ with your voice starting high and dropping. Can you hear the difference?
Here is another pair from this lesson: châi — that is a falling tone. And mâi — also a falling tone. But khàawp in ขอบคุณ has a low tone — your voice stays down. Listen: khàawp-khun.
Do not try to master all five Thai tones right now. Just notice: pitch matters. We will practise more tones every lesson.

textBlocks.heading: Key language
lines: ครับ | khráp | high tone — voice rises sharply
ค่ะ | khâ | falling tone — voice starts high and drops
ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | low tone on khàawp — voice stays low

textBlocks.heading: Practice
lines: Tone echo: I say ครับ khráp. Repeat it. Now I say ค่ะ khâ. Repeat it. Can you feel the difference?
Minimal pair choice: I say two words. Which one is ครับ and which is ค่ะ? Point to the answer.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Show tone direction arrows next to the transliteration to visualize pitch movement.
teachingVisuals: Two large cards: khráp with upward arrow, khâ with downward arrow.
Simple pitch contour line showing high vs falling vs low.
teacherCues: Model each tone slowly and clearly.
Use hand gestures to show pitch direction — up for high, down for falling, flat-low for low.
visualStrategy.rationale: Tone arrows and pitch contour lines teach better than any photograph here.

slides.title: Roleplay
speakerNotes: First polite exchange at a reception desk. You approach, greets, confirms something, and thanks the staff.
You | ขอโทษครับ สวัสดีครับ | khǎaw-thôot khráp sà-wàt-dii khráp | Excuse me, hello.
Staff | สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | Hello.
You | นี่ใช่ไหมครับ | nîi châi mái khráp | Is this right?
Staff | ใช่ค่ะ | châi khâ | Yes.
You | ขอบคุณครับ | khàawp-khun khráp | Thank you.
Staff | ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ | mâi bpen rai khâ | No problem.

textBlocks.heading: Dialogue
lines: You | ขอโทษครับ สวัสดีครับ | khǎaw-thôot khráp sà-wàt-dii khráp | Excuse me, hello.
Staff | สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | Hello.
You | นี่ใช่ไหมครับ | nîi châi mái khráp | Is this right?
Staff | ใช่ค่ะ | châi khâ | Yes.
You | ขอบคุณครับ | khàawp-khun khráp | Thank you.
Staff | ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ | mâi bpen rai khâ | No problem.

thaiFocus.thai: ขอโทษครับ สวัสดีครับ
นี่ใช่ไหมครับ
ขอบคุณครับ
ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Support the roleplay recording with clean turn-taking.
teachingVisuals: First polite exchange at a reception desk. You approach, greets, confirms something, and thanks the staff.
visualStrategy.rationale: The dialogue itself is the teaching focus.

slides.title: Recap
speakerNotes: Look away and try: how do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it before looking back.
What is the difference between ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ? One closes warmly, one repairs politely.
Someone says ขอโทษ to you. What do you say back? ไม่เป็นไร.
What do ครับ and ค่ะ do? They are the polite endings that tell the listener how your line should land.
Which two words answer the idea — yes or no? ใช่ and ไม่ใช่.

textBlocks.heading: What you can now do
lines: Look away and try: how do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it before looking back.
What is the difference between ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ? One closes warmly, one repairs politely.
Someone says ขอโทษ to you. What do you say back? ไม่เป็นไร.
What do ครับ and ค่ะ do? They are the polite endings that tell the listener how your line should land.
Which two words answer the idea — yes or no? ใช่ and ไม่ใช่.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Finish with a simple, memorable checklist.
teachingVisuals: Look away and try: how do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it before looking back.
What is the difference between ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ? One closes warmly, one repairs politely.
Someone says ขอโทษ to you. What do you say back? ไม่เป็นไร.
What do ครับ and ค่ะ do? They are the polite endings that tell the listener how your line should land.
Which two words answer the idea — yes or no? ใช่ and ไม่ใช่.
teacherCues: Use this slide as the summary.
Keep the ending calm and uncluttered.
visualStrategy.rationale: A recap slide should be compact and memorable.

slides.title: Closing
speakerNotes: By the end, you can open, repair, thank, and close a tiny first-contact exchange politely.
Next: First Contact and Courtesy continues.

textBlocks.heading: Takeaway
lines: By the end, you can open, repair, thank, and close a tiny first-contact exchange politely.
Next: First Contact and Courtesy continues.

visualStrategy.onScreenGoal: Give Nine a clean final slide to close the recording.
teachingVisuals: By the end, you can open, repair, thank, and close a tiny first-contact exchange politely.
Next: First Contact and Courtesy continues.
teacherCues: Use the closing as the final spoken wrap-up.
visualStrategy.rationale: The closing slide is a presenter anchor, not a new teaching beat.
